1: Efraim ro'eh ruach veroddef qadim: 'Ephraim feeds on wind and pursues the scorching east wind all day.' The east wind (qadim, the sirocco) is the hot desert wind that desiccates vegetation and brings no nourishment. Feeding on wind and pursuing what cannot sustain is the image of Israel's foreign policy — seeking alliance with Assyria and Egypt (shemen leMitzraim yuval, oil sent to Egypt as tribute) rather than relying on the covenant provision of YHWH. The political activity is literally futile: pursuing the sirocco produces heat exhaustion, not sustenance.
2: verivYHWH im Yehudah ulifqod al Ya'aqov kidrachav: 'The LORD has a case against Judah as well, and will punish Jacob according to his ways.' Riv (legal case, covenant lawsuit) is the prophetic legal form — YHWH as prosecutor presenting charges against the covenant people. The extension to Judah shows that the northern kingdom's failure is not unique; it reflects a pattern common to the whole people of Jacob. 'According to his deeds he will repay him' (kima'alalav yashiv lo) — the covenant principle of retributive proportionality: reward and punishment proportional to deeds.
3: babbeten aqav et achiv uveavlato sarah im Elohim: 'In the womb he grasped his brother's heel, and in his strength he contended with God.' Jacob's two defining acts are cited: the heel-grasping (aqav) at birth (Genesis 25:26, from which his name Jacob = heel-grasper is derived) and the wrestling with God (sarah im Elohim) at Peniel (Genesis 32:28). The Jacob story is invoked as interpretive key for Ephraim's current condition: Jacob's identity was built on grasping and contending, but also on the transformation that came through the encounter with God. Hosea uses both aspects: the grasping (negative) and the wrestling-and-being-transformed (potential positive model for Israel's needed return).
4: vayasar el malach vayukal yivk vayitchanen lo: 'He wrestled with the angel and prevailed; he wept and pleaded for the blessing.' The wrestling at Peniel (Genesis 32:24-30) is interpreted by Hosea with the addition of weeping (yivk) and pleading (yitchanen): Jacob did not merely defeat the angel but prevailed through tears and supplication. This is Hosea's model for the Israel of his time: to prevail with God is not through power or political maneuvering but through the weeping, desperate prayer of the one who knows his only hope is divine blessing. 'At Bethel he found him, and there God spoke with him' — Bethel, which Hosea elsewhere condemns (Hosea 4:15; Hosea 10:5), is also the site of genuine divine encounter (Genesis 28:12-17).
5: veYHWH Elohei Hatzvaot YHWH zikro: 'Even the LORD God of hosts — the LORD is his name to be remembered.' This parenthetical declaration of the divine name is Hosea's refocusing: the God who spoke with Jacob at Bethel is YHWH of armies, the transcendent sovereign. The 'name to be remembered' (zikro) connects to the covenant name-invocation — this God whose name Jacob claimed in his blessing (Genesis 32:29-30) is the same YHWH whom Israel has abandoned. The memorial name (zikr, from the same root as zakar, remember) calls Israel to return to remembering who their God is.
6: veattah beEloheicha tashuv chesed umishpat shemor: 'But as for you, return to your God; maintain covenant loyalty and justice, and wait for your God always.' The imperative triad: shuv (return/repent), shemor chesed umishpat (maintain covenant loyalty and justice), qaveh el Elohekha tamid (wait for your God always). Chesed (covenant loyalty/steadfast love) and mishpat (justice) are the two covenantal character marks Hosea most frequently calls for. Qaveh (wait with expectation, hope in) is the posture of covenantal patience — active expectant trust, not passive resignation (cf. Isaiah 40:31). The call is to imitate Jacob: weep, plead, wait — the posture of the contending-yet-dependent Jacob.
7: Kena'an bekado moz'nei mirma: 'Ephraim is a trader who uses dishonest scales; he loves to take advantage of others.' Kena'an (Canaan) had become a byword for merchant/trader (cf. Job 40:30; Proverbs 31:24; Zephaniah 1:11). Hosea applies the term to Ephraim — they have become as Canaanite merchants, using false weights (moz'nei mirma, deceitful scales). The covenant people have taken on the commercial ethics of the people they were supposed to displace. Ohev la'ashoq (he loves to take advantage) — the love of exploitation as character disposition.
8: vayomer Efraim ach asharthi matzati on li: 'But Ephraim says, Surely I have become rich; I have gained wealth for myself.' The self-congratulation of the successful merchant: wealth interpreted as self-justification. 'In all my hard work they will find no iniquity in me that is sin' — the claim that commercial success is moral evidence. This is the theology of prosperity as divine approval, one of the ancient world's (and the modern world's) most durable religious errors: if I am rich, I must be righteous. Hosea's entire book refutes this: covenant faithfulness to YHWH, not material prosperity, is the measure of covenantal standing.
9: va'ani YHWH Eloheicha me'Eretz Mitzraim: 'But I, the LORD your God since the land of Egypt, will make you live in tents again, as in the days of the appointed festivals.' The divine counter-claim: YHWH's identity as the Exodus God is the refutation of Ephraim's self-sufficiency claim. The promised return to tent-dwelling (ohel) may allude to the wilderness tent-time of the Exodus, or to the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) which commemorated wilderness living. Either way, YHWH will strip away the settled prosperity Ephraim credits to himself and restore the dependence-relationship of the wilderness generation.
10: vedibbarti al hanevi'im ve'anochi chazon hirbeiti: 'I spoke through the prophets; I multiplied visions and gave parables through the prophets.' YHWH's communication with Israel has been comprehensive: prophets (nevi'im), visions (chazon), and parables (dameh, similitudes) are all instruments of divine speech. Hosea's own work is included in this catalog: his marriage to Gomer was itself a living parable (dameh). The multiplied means of communication makes Israel's deafness inexcusable — YHWH did not fail to speak but used every available medium.
11: im Gilead aven ach shav hayu beggilgal shavarim zavachti: 'Is Gilead wicked? Yes, they are worthless! In Gilgal they sacrifice bulls; their altars will become like stone heaps on the furrows of the field.' Gilead (Transjordan) and Gilgal (cf. Hosea 9:15) are the two symbolic locations of sin — Gilead for its wickedness (possibly the Baal Peor incident, Num 25; or later crimes) and Gilgal for its cult sacrifice. The altars that should be means of covenant approach will become galim (stone heaps/cairns) — perhaps wordplay on gilgal (stone circle). Cult sites become rubble fields.
12: vayivrach Ya'aqov sedeh Aram vaya'avod Yisrael be'ishah: 'Jacob fled to the country of Aram, and Israel served for a wife and tended flocks for a wife.' The Jacob-Laban narrative (Gen 29-31) is invoked: the patriarch fled and served for love, accepting labor as the price of the relationship. Hosea invokes this to contrast the Jacob who worked and waited with the Ephraim who exploits and takes. The ancestor served willingly for what he valued; his descendants exploit and steal to get what they want. The patriarchal humility of service has inverted into the Canaanite exploitation of v7.
13: uvnavi he'elah YHWH et Yisrael miMitzraim: 'Through a prophet the LORD brought Israel up from Egypt, and through a prophet he was kept safe.' Moses is the prophet through whom YHWH accomplished both the exodus and the wilderness preservation. The prophetic mediation of redemption (Moses) and preservation (ongoing prophetic word in the wilderness) is the OT model for how YHWH works: through the prophetic voice. This verse justifies Hosea's own prophetic role — he stands in the Moses tradition as the voice through whom YHWH speaks covenant word to the people.
14: hikhis Efraim tamrurim vedamav alav yitosh: 'Ephraim has provoked his LORD to the deepest anger; so he will leave Ephraim's bloodguilt on him and repay him for his contempt.' Tamrurim (bitternesses, the bitterest of provocations) is the strongest word Hosea uses for Israel's sin against YHWH. Damim (bloodguilt, blood-crimes) will be 'left on him' (alav yitosh) — not removed or covered by atoning sacrifice, but allowed to remain as the evidence for judgment. The covenant that should have provided atonement for bloodguilt is the very thing Ephraim has rejected, leaving the guilt exposed and unpurged. The chapter closes as a legal indictment: the bloodguilt stands, the contempt is repaid.
